source.title: The development of temperament and character during adolescence: The processes and phases of change

confidence_rationale: Strong human longitudinal cohort (n=752, ages 12/14/16) in the Cloninger framework, which natively separates temperament (early, monoaminergically-grounded drive priors) from character (self-regulation toward personally valued, peer-shaped goals). The biphasic emancipation-then-internalization pattern is a close conceptual analogue of 'environmental window then lock'. Discounted because the BG-loop attribution is REE's and the study is psychometric, not neural.
failure_signatures: Novelty seeking, reward dependence and self-transcendence rise to age 14 then fall, while character rises monotonically -- a single-monotone-maturation model of personality is falsified; the trajectory is biphasic with a peer-emancipation inflection.

Character maturity at 16 is predicted by BOTH earlier temperament AND earlier character, whereas temperament dependability at 16 is predicted mainly by earlier temperament -- a model in which the regulatory layer is a pure function of the drive layer is unsupported.

mapping.source_claim_statement: In 752 adolescents, temperament traits (harm avoidance, novelty seeking, reward dependence, persistence) and character traits (self-directedness, cooperativeness) follow distinct trajectories from 12 to 16; character develops rapidly in adolescence to self-regulate temperament toward personally valued goals shaped by peers, with a biphasic pattern: pre-14 emancipation from adult authority and alignment to peer norms, post-14 internalization of a created identity.
mapping.ree_translation: Temperament = the early, relatively fixed limbic/drive priors of the commitment loops (REE limbic-loop value prior; Cloninger ties these to monoaminergic systems). Character = an adolescently-developing regulatory layer that re-weights how those drive priors gate commitment, calibrated by the peer environment. 'Internalization of the created identity' post-14 is the candidate cluster's LOCK: the peer-calibrated commitment policy is consolidated into a persisting structure once the social-calibration window closes.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Psychometric (Junior TCI), not neural; the basal-ganglia loop attribution is REE's overlay on Cloninger's psychobiological model. 'Internalization' is operationalised as trait-stabilisation and predictive structure, not as a measured plasticity-restriction mechanism, so it evidences the existence of a window-then-stabilise pattern, not the lock mechanism per se.
mapping.source_context: Longitudinal population-based US cohort, n=752, Junior Temperament and Character Inventory at ages 12, 14, 16; hierarchical linear modelling of age and gender variance.

confidence_components.notes: Large human longitudinal sample, established instrument and theory. Mapping fidelity good: Cloninger's temperament/character split maps almost directly onto drive-prior vs commitment-regulation. Transfer risk low for the human-personality claim; the neural-loop overlay carries the residual risk.
